comments: This paper presents a clear, well-motivated and technically sound generalization of convolutional neural networks to exploit arbitrary discrete symmetry groups (G-CNNs). The authors provide a concise mathematical framework (definitions of feature maps on groups, G-correlation, equivariance proofs), describe efficient implementation strategies by reducing G-convolutions to filter indexing + planar convolutions, derive backpropagation rules, and report empirical gains on rotated MNIST and CIFAR-10 relative to competitive baselines. Strengths: strong theoretical clarity and rigor; practical implementation guidance; convincing empirical improvements while keeping parameter count comparable; thoughtful discussion of pooling/subsampling on subgroups, and extension directions (reflections, hexagonal lattices, 3D). Weaknesses / suggestions: experimental evaluation is somewhat limited in scope — CIFAR-10 and rotated MNIST are informative but broader testing on larger varied real-world datasets (and measured runtime/throughput comparisons) would strengthen claims about negligible overhead and practical applicability. The paper notes a discrepancy when reproducing the All-CNN-C baseline, which raises minor concerns about experimental exactness; an ablation study isolating effects of parameter sharing vs. increased feature-map size and the interaction with common regularizers/data augmentation would be helpful. The approach as presented focuses on discrete groups (p4, p4m); discussion or experiments on approximating continuous rotations or handling interpolation artifacts would improve applicability to more general rotation invariance. Minor points: reporting wall-clock training/inference cost, memory footprint, and releasing reference code would increase reproducibility. Overall, this is an original, well-executed contribution that meaningfully advances inductive biases for CNNs and is likely to have high impact; I recommend acceptance / wide dissemination.
